frameworks.name: normative / optimal decision theory
frameworks.one_line: Derives optimal policy for general multi-alternative choice problems.

frameworks.name: bounded evidence accumulation
frameworks.one_line: Accumulation to nonlinear time-dependent bounds rather than fixed thresholds.

frameworks.name: divisive normalization
frameworks.one_line: Implements optimal policy via normalization with fixed bounds plus urgency.

frameworks.name: irrational choice phenomena (IIA, regularity, similarity)
frameworks.one_line: Behaviors usually deemed irrational are reproduced by the optimal circuit.

core_question: What is the optimal decision policy when choosing among multiple alternatives, and can it be implemented by a simple neural circuit?
key_assumptions: evidence is accumulated over time toward decision bounds
the decision problem admits a normative Bayes-optimal solution
neural implementation uses divisive normalization plus internal noise
geometric symmetry of optimal bounds permits a simplified circuit
manipulations: number of alternatives in the choice set
presence of divisive normalization and internal variability in the model
key_findings: optimal policy requires accumulation to nonlinear, time-dependent bounds
bounds are symmetric, allowing a simple normalization circuit with fixed bounds and an urgency signal
model reproduces decision-neuron responses and Hick's-law RT scaling with alternative count
with normalization and noise, model accounts for similarity effect and violations of IIA and regularity
interpretations: so-called irrational choice phenomena emerge naturally from a normatively optimal circuit
decision-related neural responses can be understood as the implementation of an optimal multi-alternative policy
